transcript.presenting_concern: Initial intake — history gathering, diagnostic impressions, treatment planning

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Nathan, thank you for coming in today. This is our initial intake session, so I'm going to ask you a lot of questions to get a full picture of what's bringing you in and what might be most helpful. Nothing is off limits, but you're always free to say "I'd rather not discuss that" and we'll move on. Sound okay?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah, that's fine. I probably should have done this years ago honestly.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Well, you're here now, and that matters. Before we dive in, let me explain the structure. We'll spend about forty-five minutes together today. I'll ask about what's bringing you in, your history — personal, family, medical — and then toward the end we'll talk about what treatment might look like. I'll also have you fill out a couple of brief questionnaires. Any questions before we begin?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No, let's just... let's get into it. I've been putting this off long enough.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Alright. Let's start with what prompted you to reach out. What's been going on?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It's... a lot of things piling up. The main thing is I can't sleep. I haven't slept through the night in probably four months. I'm lying awake until 2, 3 AM, my mind just racing about work. Then I'm exhausted during the day, snapping at my wife and kids, can't focus in meetings. I'm forgetting things — showed up to a parent-teacher conference on the wrong day last week. My wife finally said "you need to talk to someone because I can't keep being the one you take this out on."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That sounds exhausting — both the insomnia itself and the toll it's taking on your relationships and your functioning. Let me unpack that a bit. Tell me about the racing thoughts at night. What are they about?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Work. Always work. I'm a VP at a software company — I manage about eighty people across four teams. We're in the middle of a major restructuring — which is corporate-speak for layoffs — and I have to decide who stays and who goes. Every night I'm running through names, scenarios, budget numbers. "If I cut this team, we lose this capability. If I cut that person, they have a new baby. If I cut this other person, they've been with us twelve years and they trusted me when I told them their job was secure." It's like I can't turn off the decision-making engine.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It sounds like there's a significant moral weight to these decisions — it's not just strategic calculations, it's people's lives and livelihoods.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Exactly. I keep thinking I'm going to ruin someone's life. Some of these people — I hired them personally. I recruited them away from other companies. I told them this was a great place to build a career, and now I'm the one who has to look them in the eye and say "we don't need you anymore." How do you sleep after that?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: When did this start — was there a specific trigger or did it build gradually?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The sleep thing started about four months ago when the layoff planning began — when my boss pulled me into the first meeting about "headcount reduction targets." I remember driving home that night and just feeling this weight in my chest. But if I'm being honest, I've been running pretty hot for a couple years. Like, high stress, working twelve-hour days, never really disconnecting. The layoff thing was just the straw that broke it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: "Running hot for a couple of years" — describe what that looks like day to day.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Constant sense of urgency. Like everything is a fire. Checking email first thing when I wake up — before I even get out of bed, before I say good morning to my wife. Last thing at night. Checking Slack on weekends, during dinners, at my kid's basketball games. Feeling like if I'm not on top of every single thing, something will catastrophically fall apart. And the irritability — God, my fuse is so short now. I yelled at my thirteen-year-old last week because she left her backpack in the hallway. The HALLWAY. And the look on her face... she looked scared of me. That really got to me. I'm not that guy. Or I wasn't that guy.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That moment with your daughter — the fear you saw on her face — tell me more about what that brought up for you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] My dad was a yeller. Not violent, but... loud. Unpredictable. You never knew what version of him you'd get when he walked in the door. I swore I'd never be that father. And here I am, my kid is flinching because dad came home in a mood. That's when I knew something was really wrong — when I saw myself becoming him.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a really powerful self-awareness, Nathan. And it clearly matters deeply to you — being a different kind of father than what you experienced. We'll come back to your family history in a moment. Beyond the irritability and insomnia, are you noticing changes in appetite, energy, interest in activities you used to enjoy?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Appetite is weird — I'm not hungry during the day. I'll skip breakfast, have maybe half a sandwich at lunch at my desk. Then at night, after the kids go to bed, I'm standing at the fridge eating whatever I can find. Chips, leftovers, ice cream. Like my body suddenly remembers it needs food. Energy is tanked — I'm running on caffeine. Four, sometimes five cups of coffee. By 3 PM I'm in a fog regardless. Activities... [sigh] I used to golf every Saturday morning with my college roommate and two other guys. We've been doing it for fifteen years. I haven't played in two months because I can't justify taking four hours away from work — even on a Saturday. My friends have stopped asking me to join because I always say no. Rob texted last week and just said "we miss you, man" and I didn't even respond.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So you're withdrawing from activities and social connections that used to bring you enjoyment and restoration. The golf, friends reaching out and you declining, not responding to texts — do you miss those things?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Yeah. I miss who I was three years ago. I used to be fun. I used to make my wife laugh. I used to be present with my kids — coaching basketball, building LEGO sets on Sundays. Now I'm physically there but mentally I'm always somewhere else. My wife says it's like living with a ghost. She can see me but I'm not really there.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a vivid description — "living with a ghost." How is your relationship with your wife currently?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Strained. We don't fight often because I don't have the energy to fight. It's more like... cold distance. We're roommates managing logistics — who's picking up the kids, what's for dinner. We haven't been on a date in months. The physical intimacy is basically gone. She tried to initiate last month and I just... couldn't. Didn't have it in me. I saw the hurt on her face and felt terrible, but I couldn't make myself feel something I didn't feel.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Loss of sexual interest is actually a common symptom of both depression and chronic stress. It's not a character failure — it's your body and mind being depleted. Tell me about your children. You mentioned a thirteen-year-old?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Olivia is thirteen, sophomore in high school next year. And Jake is nine. Olivia has noticed something's off — she asked my wife if I'm "mad at her" because I'm always short with her. Jake is younger and more resilient but even he's said "dad, how come you never play with me anymore?" That one... that was another wake-up call.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Nathan, I want to ask some important screening questions. I ask these of everyone regardless of what they're presenting with. Have you had thoughts of hurting yourself or that life isn't worth living?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. Not like that. I've had thoughts of just wanting to get off the treadmill — like quitting my job and disappearing to a cabin somewhere. Just vanishing. No phone, no email, no decisions. But not suicide. That's more about escape from the pressure than ending things. I want my life — I just don't want it at this speed.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Thank you for being clear about that distinction. That desire for escape is very common in burnout — the fantasy of just walking away from all responsibilities. I want to acknowledge it's not the same as suicidal ideation, but I also want to ask: have you had any thoughts of harming others?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No, absolutely not. I mean, I get frustrated, but no. Nothing like that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Do you use alcohol or any substances to manage the stress or help with sleep?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I've been drinking more. Used to be a glass of wine with dinner — social, relaxed. Now it's two or three glasses most nights. Sometimes four on a bad night. Started before the insomnia as a wind-down thing, but now I think it's become a crutch. Like, I need it to turn my brain off. I noticed that last Tuesday I had a particularly hard day — had to tell a team lead their position was being eliminated — and I came home and poured a whiskey before I even took off my jacket. That felt automatic in a way that scared me a little.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I appreciate your honesty and your self-awareness about that. The automaticity you're describing — reaching for alcohol before conscious decision-making — is worth monitoring. Two to three glasses most nights, occasionally four, is above recommended limits and the pattern of using it as emotional regulation is concerning. We'll keep an eye on that as part of our work together. Any other substances? Cannabis, sleep aids, anything else?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I tried melatonin for a while — didn't do much. Sometimes I'll take a Benadryl if I'm desperate. No cannabis, nothing else.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How about caffeine? You mentioned four to five cups of coffee.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah, throughout the day. Usually my last cup is around 3 or 4 PM. I know that's probably not helping the sleep thing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: We'll circle back to that when we talk about sleep hygiene. Now I want to get some background history. You mentioned seeing a therapist in your twenties — tell me about that.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah, after my dad died. I was twenty-six. He had a heart attack — sudden, no warning. One day he was there, next day he wasn't. I was a mess. Couldn't eat, couldn't focus at work. My girlfriend at the time — now my wife, actually — basically insisted I go talk to someone. So I saw a guy, Dr. Patterson, for maybe six sessions. It was helpful. He helped me process the grief. Then I felt better and stopped going.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Tell me more about your father. You mentioned he was a yeller — unpredictable. What was your relationship with him like?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Complicated. He was a hard man. Good provider — we never wanted for anything material. But emotionally he was... unavailable most of the time, and volatile the rest of it. He worked in construction management, came home exhausted and angry most nights. Mom walked on eggshells around him. We all did. When he was in a good mood, he was amazing — funny, generous, would take us fishing. But you never knew which dad you were getting. So you learned to be quiet, be good, don't rock the boat. Perform well. Get good grades. Don't give him a reason to blow up.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And how do you think that shaped you?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] I became a perfectionist. An overachiever. If I excelled, I was safe. Good grades, good athlete, good employee. If you're the best, no one has a reason to be angry at you. I think... I think I've been running that program my whole life. Perform at the highest level or something bad will happen. And now I'm forty-five and still running it, except now the bar is managing eighty people through layoffs without making a single mistake, and that's literally impossible.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a really important insight. The adaptive strategy of your childhood — be perfect, perform, stay safe — is now the engine driving your burnout. It was brilliant survival as a child. It's unsustainable as a middle-aged executive. How was your relationship with your mother?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Close, in a way. She was warm but... passive. She didn't protect us from him. I don't blame her — she was scared too. But there was a part of me that wished she'd stand up to him. She managed the house, managed our emotions, managed dad. She was the peacekeeper. After dad died, she actually blossomed. Started traveling, made friends, took painting classes. Like she'd been in a cage and didn't know it until the door opened.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You mentioned your mom was on antidepressants. Tell me more about mental health history in your family.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Mom's been on Lexapro for... I don't know, maybe twenty years? Started sometime while Dad was alive. She's pretty open about it. My younger brother Mike has anxiety — he's been on medication for that since his thirties. He's more visibly anxious — he's the one who has panic attacks, can't fly on planes, that kind of thing. I always thought I was the "together" one. The one who didn't need help.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The "together" one. The performer. The one who pushes through.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. [quiet laugh] Lot of good it's done me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Any history of substance use disorders in the family? Given what you're sharing about your own drinking increasing, it's relevant to ask.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My dad drank. Beer mostly. I don't know if he was an alcoholic in the clinical sense, but he drank every night and it made him meaner. So yeah. I'm aware of the irony — I'm becoming the man I swore I wouldn't become. Different substance, but same pattern. Come home stressed, reach for a drink, get more irritable.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That awareness is important. And the fact that you're catching it now, before it progresses further, puts you in a much better position than if you waited another five years. Any other family history I should know about? Bipolar, psychosis, suicide?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Not that I know of. Dad's side was pretty stoic — "men don't talk about feelings" type of family. So who knows what was going on undiagnosed. Mom's side is more open. No one else with mental health issues that I'm aware of.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let me ask about your medical history. Any chronic health conditions, past surgeries, ongoing issues?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Pretty healthy overall. I had my appendix out at nineteen. Last physical was about a year ago — cholesterol was a little high, blood pressure was borderline. Doctor said "lose ten pounds, eat better, exercise more." Haven't done any of that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Are you currently taking any medications?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. Just the occasional Benadryl for sleep.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Alright. I want to switch gears and do a couple of brief screening measures. These are standardized questionnaires that help me quantify what you're experiencing. The first is the PHQ-9 — it measures depressive symptoms over the past two weeks. I'll read the items and you tell me how often you've experienced each one: not at all, several days, more than half the days, or nearly every day.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Over the past two weeks, how often have you had little interest or pleasure in doing things?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: More than half the days. The things I used to enjoy — golf, cooking, playing with the kids — I just don't want to do them. It feels like too much effort.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Several days. Not every day, but maybe three or four days a week I have this heavy, flat feeling. Like nothing matters or everything is pointless.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day. That's the big one. Every single night is a battle.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Feeling tired or having little energy?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day. I'm running on fumes and coffee.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Poor appetite or overeating?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: More than half the days. The not-eating-during-the-day, bingeing-at-night thing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Feeling bad about yourself — or that you are a failure or have let yourself or your family down?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] More than half the days. Especially the letting my family down part. I feel like I'm failing as a father and a husband. I can see it happening and I can't seem to stop it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Trouble concentrating on things, such as reading the newspaper or watching television?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: More than half the days. I can't focus in meetings. I have to reread emails three times. My wife was talking to me last night and I realized I hadn't heard a single word. She noticed and walked away.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Moving or speaking so slowly that other people could have noticed? Or the opposite — being so fidgety or restless that you've been moving around a lot more than usual?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The restless one. I can't sit still. I'm pacing during phone calls, tapping my foot in meetings, checking my phone compulsively. My body feels wired even when my brain is exhausted.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And the last one — thoughts that you would be better off dead or of hurting yourself in some way?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Not at all. Like I said — I want out of the pressure, not out of life.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Your total score on the PHQ-9 is 18, which falls in the moderately severe range for depression. Now let me do the GAD-7 — this measures anxiety. Same format. Over the past two weeks, how often have you been feeling nervous, anxious, or on edge?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day. There's this constant hum of dread. Like something bad is about to happen even when nothing specific is wrong.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Not being able to stop or control worrying?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day. That's the nighttime thing — I literally cannot turn it off. I've tried deep breathing, counting, all the tricks. The worry just keeps going.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Worrying too much about different things?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day. Work, family, health, finances — it cycles through everything. I can address one worry and three more appear.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Trouble relaxing?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day. I can't remember the last time I felt truly relaxed. Even on vacation last Christmas, I was checking email from the hotel.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Being so restless that it's hard to sit still?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: More than half the days. Same as before — the pacing, the restlessness.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Becoming easily annoyed or irritable?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Nearly every day. Everyone around me would confirm that. My wife, my kids, my direct reports. I'm snapping at people over nothing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Feeling afraid, as if something awful might happen?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Several days. It's more of a vague sense of dread than a specific fear. Like something is going to go very wrong and it'll be my fault.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Your GAD-7 score is 19, which is in the severe range for generalized anxiety. So Nathan, here's what I'm seeing. Based on everything you've told me and these scores — PHQ-9 of 18, GAD-7 of 19 — you're presenting with significant symptoms of both major depressive disorder and generalized anxiety disorder. These are co-occurring, which is very common, especially in high-performing professionals under chronic stress. The picture also includes features of occupational burnout and a pattern of increasing alcohol use that may be complicating things.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So it's as bad as I thought. Or worse, maybe.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I wouldn't use the word "bad" — I'd say you're experiencing significant psychological distress that's been building for years, and it's now manifesting in ways you can't ignore anymore. Your body and mind are telling you loudly that something needs to change. The good news — and there genuinely is good news — is that this is very treatable. What you're experiencing responds well to a combination of therapy and, potentially, medication.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. Tell me what that looks like.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I'd recommend we meet weekly to start — possibly moving to biweekly once you're more stable. Our work would focus on several areas. First, cognitive behavioral strategies for the worry and the perfectionism — helping you identify and challenge the thought patterns that are keeping you stuck in high-alert mode. The "if I'm not perfect, catastrophe will follow" belief system that served you as a kid but is now burning you out.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Second, behavioral activation — systematically rebuilding the activities and connections you've dropped. Golf, friends, quality time with your kids, physical activity. These aren't luxuries; they're essential parts of your mental health infrastructure, and they've eroded.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It's hard because every time I think about doing something for myself, there's this voice that says "you should be working."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And that voice is exactly what we'll work with. That's the internalized belief that your only value is productivity. Third area: sleep hygiene and relaxation training. We'll address the insomnia directly with evidence-based techniques — cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia, or CBT-I, is actually more effective long-term than sleep medication. But it takes some work.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: What about the alcohol piece?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Fourth area — we'll monitor your alcohol use and work on developing alternative coping strategies. Right now alcohol is serving a function — it's your off switch. We need to help you develop other off switches so alcohol doesn't become a bigger problem than it already is. Given your family history with your father's drinking, being proactive about this is wise.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. I don't want to go down that road.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I'd also strongly recommend you see our psychiatrist, Dr. Patel, for a medication evaluation. A short-term sleep aid could help break the insomnia cycle while we build your behavioral strategies. And an SSRI or SNRI could address both the anxiety and the depression simultaneously. With scores this elevated, therapy alone may be slower than therapy plus medication.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [sigh] I've always resisted medication. It feels like... I don't know. A crutch. Like admitting I can't handle my own life. Like I'm weak.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I want to examine that belief with you for a moment. You manage eighty people. If one of your direct reports came to you and said "I'm not sleeping, I'm anxious all the time, I can't concentrate, my family relationships are suffering" — what would you tell them?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'd tell them to see a doctor. Take care of themselves. That no job is worth destroying your health over.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Would you think they were weak?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. Of course not. I'd think they were being responsible.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So the standards you apply to others — compassion, practicality, getting help when you need it — you don't apply to yourself. That's the perfectionism at work. It says "other people can need help, but I should be able to handle everything alone." Medication isn't a crutch — it's a tool that can give your brain the chemical foundation to do the cognitive work more effectively. Just like an anti-inflammatory helps you do physical therapy more effectively after an injury.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] Fair point. I'd tell any of my reports to see a doctor. You're right. I hold myself to a different standard.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That double standard is one of the things we'll work on. So — would you be willing to see Dr. Patel? I can have our coordinator set up an appointment.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Yeah. Okay. I'll try it. If it doesn't work or the side effects are bad, I can stop, right?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Absolutely. It's not a permanent commitment. It's a trial, and you remain in control of the decision. Alright, let's talk about immediate changes — things you can start this week before our next session. First, the alcohol. I'm not asking you to quit, but I'd like you to start tracking — just write down what you drink each day. No judgment, just data. Awareness is the first step.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I can do that. I'll put it in my phone.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Second — the caffeine. Can you commit to no coffee after 2 PM? That five-hour half-life means your 4 PM coffee is still half-active in your system at 9 PM.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's... going to be hard at 3 PM when I'm fading, but okay. I'll try switching to decaf in the afternoon.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Third, and this is the big one: can you commit to leaving work by 6 PM at least three days this week? Not every day — three days. And when you leave, I want you to put your phone in a drawer until after the kids are in bed.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Three days... that's going to be hard with everything going on. The restructuring decisions are due to the board in two weeks. But I... [pause] okay. I'll try. Three days. Phone in the drawer.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Hard is different from impossible. And Nathan — your daughter who's scared of your moods? Your son who wants you to play with him? Your wife who's sleeping in the same bed as a ghost? They need three evenings. You can give them that.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [voice cracking slightly] Yeah. You're right. They deserve better than what I've been giving them.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: They do. And so do you. You deserve to sleep. You deserve to feel present in your own life. You've been running a program written by a scared kid in a volatile household, and it served you well for a long time. But you're not that kid anymore, and the program is now doing more harm than good. That's what we're going to work on — rewriting that code.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [soft laugh] You're speaking my language with the software metaphor.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I chose it deliberately. Okay — one more thing. Last question of the intake: what does success look like to you? If our work together goes well, what's different six months from now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] I'm sleeping. I'm present with my family. Olivia isn't afraid of me. Jake and I are playing catch again. My wife and I are... connected. I'm still working hard — I'm not going to become a slacker — but I'm working at a sustainable pace. I respond to Rob's text. I play golf on Saturday mornings. And I don't need three glasses of wine to get through an evening.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a beautiful vision, Nathan. And it's achievable. Every piece of that is something we can work toward concretely. I'm going to hold that vision with you, and when things feel hard in our work together — and they will — we'll come back to it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr. Chen. I feel... relieved, honestly. Like it's not just rattling around in my own head anymore. Someone else can see the whole picture now and tell me it's fixable.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It is fixable. You've already taken the hardest step — walking through that door. I'll get you set up with Dr. Patel for the medication evaluation, and I'll see you next Thursday, same time. Between now and then: track the alcohol, cut caffeine after 2 PM, three evenings home by 6 with the phone away. Deal?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Deal. Same time next week. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're welcome. Take care of yourself this week, Nathan. You've earned it.
